title: She Always Had Money
summary: Interviewed on November 17, 2010, Myrtle Gonza Glascoe, born and raised in Washington D.C., opens by saying her family was not political and nothing in her childhood pointed to the movement, except that she was always too frank, and recalls hauling groceries by bicycle for a quarter and negotiating carnival money from her father.

title: I Went to School and I Partied
summary: She recounts graduating from Dunbar High School and Howard University without any political spark, classmates remembering she always had something going, catching a classmate plagiarizing an Esquire essay, and her easy facility for taking notes and earning excellent grades while knowing nothing of the politics around her.

title: They Asked Me to Leave
summary: As a health and physical education major she took her first job at a Catholic boarding school for young Black women, let the girls dance to the records they wanted, diagrammed for a student how she could get pregnant, stopped attending mass, and was told by the Mother Superior and principal that she had to go by that night, negotiating pay for the rest of the year.

title: A Rod of Steel Up My Back
summary: After earning a social work degree from the University of Pennsylvania and working at Rosewood State Hospital outside Baltimore, a coworker who chaired Baltimore CORE brought her to a meeting; refusing to walk the picket line because she did not believe in nonviolence, she did support work until a greasy counterman ordered her group out of the White Coffee Pot, an anger she felt all the way home that turned her toward the movement.

title: These Are the Youth and They Need Support
summary: She describes joining the Route 40 demonstrations, learning that going to jail was a deliberate choice people prepared for with a toothbrush, driving Howard students like Courtland Cox and Bill Mahoney to actions, and explaining that her refusal of nonviolence was a gut response because she had attended no sessions and knew nothing of its philosophy.

title: Like I Was Eating Dinner Every Day
topic: From Baltimore to San Francisco SNCC
summary: She recounts leaving Baltimore in 1962 for Los Angeles, reconnecting with CORE, then through a national CORE officer she had befriended meeting Mike Miller, the SNCC support chair in San Francisco; she moved north, devoured the papers Mike brought back from the South, and met Lawrence Guyot, Ivanhoe Donaldson, and Fannie Lou Hamer, whom she drove around and who pulled out her tobacco for a good chew.

title: There's No Reason You Can't Go
summary: Sitting on a committee to choose people for the 1965 summer after many white volunteers had stayed on, she decided to go herself, prompted by a young Chinese coworker, recently widowed, who asked why she could not go and ticked off that she had no bills, no husband, no children, and recalls finding in Arkansas the same joy of dancing she had known visiting cousins in southern Maryland.

title: Stay in Your Seat
summary: Becoming Director of the Freedom School in West Helena, Arkansas, she ran it as a day camp drawing on years of camp work, and names as the summer's most important lesson the guidance from Mike and the project director to watch, listen, and learn rather than lead, to attend community meetings but write down her thoughts and stay in her seat, doing voter registration as one of two Black volunteers among nine.

title: Feces Floating Down the Hall
topic: The Turner School and the Boycott
summary: Describing the method of helping people solve their own problems, she tells the Jones Ridge, Arkansas case where families at Turner Elementary, plagued by toilets backing up and feces floating down the hall whenever it rained, were brought by her coworker to take their complaint to the Little Rock Health Department, found the pipes had been laid uphill, and gained the confidence that grew into a school boycott to force desegregation in the summer of 1966.

title: All of My Kids Hate White Folks
summary: She describes holding 1500 of 1800 Black children out of the segregated school for six weeks in 1966 until the district desegregated, her own growing doubt that they should be entering hostile white schools rather than building their own, and Gertrude Jackson telling her years later that all her children hated white folks after being barred from the band and treated with condescension.

title: Sugar in the Cotton Picker
summary: She talks about the Jackson family of Marvell, the project's center, Gertrude and Earlis Jackson with their eleven children, his brother James, and a cousin who owned the store, all staunch for change, and how when sugar was put in the gas tank of their cotton picker the brothers calmly took the engine apart, cleaned it, reassembled it, and kept working.

title: This Is Your Idea, Not Mine
topic: Making a Leader Stand on Her Own
summary: She recounts realizing that Gertrude Jackson kept planting ideas on her to present at meetings, and how she insisted Mrs. Jackson present her own ideas so the community would know she was the one who had them, a tense first attempt that grew until Mrs. Jackson pulled people together, got them organized, and helped found a community center that still exists.

title: Building Blocks to Being in Charge
summary: She frames her goal as creating a process where local people got in touch with their own capability and developed the confidence to act on their own, contrasting Gertrude Jackson, who learned to trust herself and connected with a Connecticut woman to send all her girls to college, with her husband and others who wanted to lead but did not yet trust themselves.

title: Stuff Inside Me I Didn't Know I Had
topic: White Workers Leave the Black Communities
summary: She describes her Jewish coworker Howard Himmelbaum, with whom she worked well, returning from a New Orleans meeting where SNCC decided white workers should leave the Black communities to work in white ones, and how, scared at first, she discovered after he left a capacity to make decisions and generate ideas she had not known were in her.

title: Not Seen as Someone in Charge
topic: Authority, Race, and the Man-Woman Role
summary: She reflects that she had been deferring to Howard in a traditional man-woman role she had not recognized, and analyzes the deeper challenge of getting Black people to act for themselves without seeing a college-educated outsider with a master's degree as an authority figure, a difficulty sharper still for a white worker, while noting Howard's willingness to reflect on how he affected the community.

title: It Gets Inside You
topic: Activism and the First Head Start
summary: Leaving SNCC in the fall of 1967 broke and taking a Head Start job, she explains that becoming an activist is permanent, and recounts how Mississippi's 1965 program, the first Head Start in the country, was set up by returning white volunteers but, built on the wheels and infrastructure of the movement, began to destruct that infrastructure once paid jobs and board decisions divided people who had organized together for free.

title: I Had the Job Because I Had the Job
topic: Senator Stennis and the Painful Politics
summary: She describes how Senator Stennis fought the Head Start program because staff kept doing movement work, bringing it to repeated dead stops over jobs and board seats until coworkers who had struggled alongside her grew angry that she still held a job, a strain so painful she finally decided to quit each time the program stopped and wait until it returned.

title: What's in Your Suitcase
topic: Tougaloo and the Small Group in Process
summary: She recounts teaching at Tougaloo College from 1969 to 1972 under Dr. Ernst Borinski, becoming a Muslim in the Nation and sharing how to eat to live, and developing a course called The Small Group in Process focused on knowing oneself and one's community, which she still salts into her teaching by asking students what beliefs and attitudes they carry in their suitcase into a classroom of children.

title: In Support of the Work
topic: West Point and the Food Stamps
summary: She clarifies her role in John Buffington's West Point, Mississippi project, where she was not on her own but supported work already underway, attended meetings, helped with the restaurant John had started, and ran the food stamps, buying all the food and drawing complaints for not buying what people wanted, doing no voter registration there as she had in Arkansas.

title: If He Did Not Say No, I Was Gone
summary: She tells how she was invited in 1985 to be Founding Director of the Avery Research Center for African American History and Culture, housed in the building of the Avery School, founded in 1865 as the first academic high school for Black people in Charleston and reputed within the Black community to favor light-skinned students, and how graduates lobbied to reclaim it as an institution to support Black education.

title: Bringing the Carawan Collection Home
topic: Spending the Budget to Build It
summary: She describes inheriting a deteriorating building and a single office, choosing to spend her leftover first-year budget by purchasing Guy and Candy Carawan's collection of John's Island music, art, and photographs after they spoke of the islanders wanting the materials available to them, and planning a conference on the work of Black women in South Carolina to show the center was alive.

title: I'm Not Going to Ask Nobody
summary: She recounts justifying each year's budget to South Carolina decision-makers by listing what she had done and intended to do, partnering with a Black Charlestonian legislator and an earlier graduates' commitment from the legislature, and her activism of telling her boss David, the Director of Libraries, her plans so that unless he said no outright she went ahead and spent every dollar on books, tapes, equipment, and a copy of Du Bois's papers.

title: This Place Has a Life of Its Own
topic: Leaving Avery, Then the Education Course
summary: She explains that the center took on a life of its own that the college did not want, preferring it sit as a showpiece they had given Black people, so she left rather than just move papers, and then in the School of Education was handed a syllabus about only white people, which she immediately revised to introduce Native, African, and Asian peoples without asking permission.

title: On One Hand African, On the Other American
summary: Asked about her research on W.E.B. Du Bois and Booker T. Washington, she discusses her published work on the evolution of Du Bois's educational thought, a paper at Greeley, Colorado on his role founding the NAACP, and her reading of two-ness as the African and American halves children must hold, along with Du Bois's later turn on the Talented Tenth toward the most gifted coming forward to serve.

title: It's Alive and Well and It Eats on Us
summary: She frames the struggle as preparing young people to know who they are while seeing clearly the contradictions of a society whose desegregated education does not serve them, naming Marcus Garvey's Universal Negro Improvement Association calling for dual citizenship, and arguing that teachers of every race, taught to accept the innate inferiority of children of color, do not believe Black children can learn.

title: My Child Has to Learn How to Think
summary: She recounts pressing future teachers who came back wanting to recite book answers, refusing to set a memorization test and instead asking them to apply what they read to what is happening in schools, insisting that a teacher who cannot think cannot teach her child, and that this is true for all children, including poor white ones.

title: You Will Never Get a Husband Down There
summary: She closes by recalling that she told no one she was going South and just went, that her mother arrived for a visit transformed into an activist whose nurses' group sent down clothes, yet greeted her at home with the warning that she would never get a husband there, a mother she later understood to have been terrified and wishing her on the path to marriage and children.
